front: Poem: the Cross narrates Christ's crucifixion from its own perspective, describing itself as covered in gems and gold and bleeding on its right side
back: The Dream of the Rood (Vercelli Book)

front: Poem: part of the text is inscribed in runic characters on the Ruthwell Cross, an 8th-century stone monument in a Scottish border town, predating any manuscript
back: The Dream of the Rood (Vercelli Book)

front: Poem: variously attributed to Caedmon or Cynewulf; the attribution to Caedmon rests on circumstantial evidence in Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People
back: The Dream of the Rood (Vercelli Book)

front: Poem: opens with the narrator's 'best of dreams' vision of the Cross; the poem has two parts — the narrator's vision, then the Cross speaking in first person
back: The Dream of the Rood (Vercelli Book)

front: Poem: complete text discovered in 1822 as part of the Vercelli Book
back: The Dream of the Rood (Vercelli Book)

front: Poem: a character is renamed Cyriacus after finding three crosses underground while helping the protagonist
work: Cynewulf's Poems (Elene, The Fates of the Apostles, etc.)

front: Author: one of the only named Old English poets; signed works such as Elene, Juliana, Christ II, and The Fates of the Apostles using runic letters woven into the text
work: Cynewulf's Poems (Elene, The Fates of the Apostles, etc.)

front: Manuscript: a 10th-century Old English codex that contains The Dream of the Rood, alliterative poems by Cynewulf, and a collection of prose homilies
